ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated drinkable/liquid yoghurt entry; closest defensible match is the general yogurt food product class. Liquid/drinkable form noted in classification.
ontology.foodex2_description: Yoghurt and other fermented milk products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fermented milk products including drinking yoghurt; no exact drinkable-only sub-code identified in public FoodEx2 browser as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226760005 covers yogurt as a substance; no dedicated drinking-yoghurt concept identified.

main_ingredients: live bacterial cultures (Lactobacillus bulgaricus, Streptococcus thermophilus)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve portion of plain yoghurt drink is one bottle or cup of approximately 200 ml (200 g), commonly consumed as a breakfast accompaniment or snack.

nutrient_source.title: Plain Yoghurt Drink — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for plain yoghurt drink (cr_id D02020008). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01117 (Yogurt, plain, low fat) as the closest approved analogous entry and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for plain yoghurt drink found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 36 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for low-fat plain yoghurt (GI=36, reference food glucose=100), which is the closest approved analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (36) × available carbohydrate per serving (16.62 g/100g × 200 g serving = 33.24 g) ÷ 100 = 11.97, rounded to 11.9. Liquid form may marginally increase glycaemic response relative to solid yoghurt; this is noted as a clinical caution.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (13.63 g/100 g) in liquid form independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, despite low sodium (49 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is within acceptable limits (49 mg/100 g), but the high sugar load (13.63 g/100 g) in a beverage exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold and independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; patients with hypertension should limit intake to no more than 100–150 ml per occasion and prefer plain unsweetened yoghurt instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (13.63 g/100 g) in liquid form with zero dietary fibre causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a low GI of 36 for solid yoghurt, the liquid form with 13.63 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre accelerates glucose absorption; individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage and substitute plain unsweetened solid yoghurt, which delivers equivalent probiotic benefit with negligible added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~0.74 g/100 g) and high sugar (13.63 g/100 g) in liquid form; calcium (131 mg/100 g) and probiotics are cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content and beneficial calcium and probiotic profile are positive cardiovascular factors, but the high sugar content (13.63 g/100 g) in beverage form contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia risk with regular consumption; cardiovascular patients should limit portion size to 100 ml and prefer unsweetened variants.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Live bacterial cultures and calcium (131 mg/100 g) support gut microbiome and bone health; high sugar may promote inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fermented dairy with live cultures supports gut microbiome modulation and calcium intake is beneficial for bone protection in patients on corticosteroids, but the high sugar content (13.63 g/100 g) may promote inflammatory mediators with frequent consumption; prefer plain unsweetened yoghurt for equivalent probiotic benefit without the inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain yoghurt drink is a liquid cultured dairy beverage produced by fermenting milk with live probiotic bacterial cultures, typically Lactobacillus bulgaricus and Streptococcus thermophilus, without added flavours or sweeteners beyond the naturally occurring lactose and sugars present in the milk base. The drink has a smooth, pourable consistency, a mildly tangy flavour characteristic of fermented dairy, and a slightly creamy mouthfeel despite its low fat content (1.14 g/100 g). It is commonly consumed as a breakfast drink, post-meal digestive aid, or light snack across Singapore and internationally. Per 100 g, it provides 90 kcal, 3.34 g protein, 1.14 g fat, 16.62 g carbohydrate (of which 13.63 g is sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, 49 mg sodium, and 131 mg calcium. The high calcium content supports bone health, and the live probiotic cultures support gut microbiome diversity. Clinically, the high sugar content (13.63 g/100 g) in liquid form is a concern for Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension management, as liquid sugars are absorbed more rapidly than solid food equivalents. The glycemic index is estimated at 36 (low) based on the closest approved analogous food (low-fat plain yoghurt), but the liquid delivery form and high sugar load produce a glycemic load of approximately 11.9 per 200 ml serving, which is clinically meaningful for diabetic patients. The food is suitable for vegetarians, halal, and gluten-free diets, but is not suitable for vegans or those with dairy allergies.
common_user_queries: Is plain yoghurt drink good for diabetes?
Can I drink yoghurt if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a yoghurt drink?
What is the sugar content of plain yoghurt drink?
Is yoghurt drink good for gut health?
Is yoghurt drink suitable for weight loss?
Yoghurt drink vs solid yoghurt — which is healthier?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D02020008, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01117 (low-fat plain yoghurt) as the closest approved analogous entry. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for low-fat plain yoghurt (GI=36). All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0 g/100 g reported). Iron_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0 mg/100 g reported). These are confirmed zero values from the approved source, not missing data. 2. saturated_fat_g (0.74 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01117 (Yogurt, plain, low fat) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; this is an approved-source adaptation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific plain yoghurt drink are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D02020008 as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI of 36 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (low-fat plain yoghurt); no direct GI entry for drinkable plain yoghurt was identified. The liquid form may marginally increase glycaemic response relative to solid yoghurt. 5. This food is a generic record (not a branded product); the description 'no added flavours' in the input is preserved but the sugar content (13.63 g/100 g) reflects naturally occurring lactose and milk sugars rather than added sucrose, consistent with the HPB source. 6. Multilingual local names are not applicable for this generic international food category; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine-specific naming obligation applies.
